title: Health Care Costs and Corporate Investment
abstract: Health care costs for U.S. employers have tripled over the past 20 years. Using firm-specific health expense data, I show that firms negatively adjust capital expenditures and R&D expenses in response to increases in health care costs. The effects are more pronounced for firms that are financially constrained, employ more high-skilled workers, and have less bargaining power relative to insurers. Furthermore, policy uncertainty surrounding health care costs is substantial and discourages capital investment. These findings suggest that an elevated level of health care costs and the associated uncertainty limit a firm’s ability to expand physically or through innovation.

author.Joy Tianjiao Tong*: Ivey Business School, University of Western Ontario,Canada

article.1 Background:

Health and longevity create enormous value for the economy (Murphy and Topel 2006). As with education and on-the-job training, health is a crucial aspect of human capital (Mushkin 1962), that affects individuals’ capacity to work and work quality. The recent COVID-19 pandemic has demonstrated the profound degree to which a health crisis can affect firms’ human capital across the board and massively disrupt the world economy. In the United States, a prominent feature of the health care system is its dependence on the private sector. Employer-sponsored health benefits cover the health care of nearly 60% of working-age Americans. In 2018, U.S. employers spent $727 billion to provide health coverage for 175 million people (Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services 2018). Health benefits are offered by most large firms, and are nontrivial parts of workers’ compensation.1What’s more, the United States has the highest health care spending per capita among all developed countries.2In 1999–2016, workers’ earnings and inflation increased cumulatively by 60% and 44%, respectively, while health insurance premiums increased by 213% (KFF 2016). With an aging population, health care costs are only expected to increase further. Despite the importance of health care costs and their link to firms’ operations, few studies have examined how employee health care costs shape corporate policies. In this paper, I explore the effect of health care costs on firms by examining whether and how these costs affect capital and R&D investment decisions.

Capital and R&D investments are important for firms’ long-term growth, innovation, and regional growth (e.g.,De Long and Summers 1991). Also, weakening domestic private investment over the past two decades (Gutiérrez and Philippon 2017) has coincided with a period of rapid growth in health care costs. However, the effect of health care costs on investment is theoretically ambiguous. Long-standing belief holds that employers pass increments in health care costs on to workers dollar for dollar in the form of lower wages (Summers 1989;Gruber 1994). According to this “full pass-through” view, a rise in health care costs should affect only labor market outcomes and household decisions, but not firms’ production and investment.3

Even if employers do not fully pass health care costs on to workers, the direction in which health care costs affect investment is unclear. On the one hand, health care costs can decrease corporate investment through several non-mutually-exclusive channels. For example, a rise in firms’ health expenses decreases internal cash, as firms need to either make higher payments to insurance companies or contribute more to their health plan funding reserves. Because external financing is costly (Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen 1988;Lamont 1997;Rauh 2006), firms reduce investment as a response. In addition, health care costs can affect investment by affecting firms’ optimal production scale or worker health. These channels predict a negative relationship between health care costs and investment.

Conversely, rising health care costs can increase investment, and one potential channel through which this could occur is by reducing worker mobility. In particular, the prevalence of employer-sponsored health benefits can create “job-lock,” where employees fear losing their current health coverage and remain in the same jobs (Madrian 1994;Garthwaite, Gross, and Notowidigdo 2014). This is because switching jobs can result in gaps in coverage or reduced health benefits;4the disparity in health benefits between jobs expands as health care costs increase. Consequently, employees stay in their current positions and are more likely to invest in firm-specific skills, accumulating human capital that enhances productivity and encourages corporate investment (see, e.g.,Belot, Boone, and Van Ours 2007;Jeffers 2023).

Therefore, it is an empirical question of how much health care costs affect investment and in what direction. However, obstacles stand in the way of establishing this relationship empirically. First, it is difficult to distinguish the effect of health care costs from other factors, such as investment opportunities, that could affect investment decisions. For example, growing firms might offer generous health plans, and at the same time, invest heavily. I address these endogeneity concerns by exploring two settings that introduce exogenous variations in the health care costs faced by employers. The two settings involve changes to health care costs in opposite directions and affect different components of the cost. An additional challenge is that data on firm-level health care spending are sparse. Consequently, I construct a data set on firm-level health benefits gathered from Form 5500 welfare benefit plan data maintained by the Department of Labor, which contains information on health insurance expenses and other health plan characteristics.

My first identification strategy is tied to state-level medical malpractice tort reforms that reduce health care costs. Tort reforms aim to lessen the ability of patients to litigate and to lower patient damages in medical malpractice cases. This reduces the incentive for health providers to engage in excessive ex ante precautionary treatment and “defensive medicine,” resulting in lower medical treatment intensity (Frakes 2012). This, in turn, reduces medical costs (Kessler and McClellan 1996) and employer-sponsored health insurance premiums (Avraham, Dafny, and Schanzenbach 2010).

For this setting, I use a difference-in-differences research design to examine corporate investment changes, exploiting the staggered change of tort reforms across states that lower health care costs. Specifically, I focus on tort reform concerningcaps on noneconomic damages; these changes restrict the amount plaintiffs can claim for physical pain and emotional suffering. A first-stage regression, using Form 5500 data, confirms that tort reforms reduce employers’ health care costs. The second stage results show that the enactment of caps on noneconomic damages increases total corporate investment by 8.9% from its average level during the sample period of 1999 to 2016. The results are robust using the efficient estimator outlined byBorusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2022)to address concerns about staggered treatment adoption for difference-in-differences research design (de Chaisemartin and d’Haultfœuille 2020). The tort reforms used in the analyses are solely for medical malpractice and are mainly lobbied for by health practitioners. Additional robustness tests show that the results are not driven by product liability reforms, state partisan status or local consumer demand. The results also hold when using a matched sample by propensity score matching or when considering time-varying industry-level shocks.

After establishing a negative relationship between health care costs and corporate investment using tort reforms, I continue to examine four channels that potentially drive the relationship between health care costs and investment. The negative effects of health care costs on investment could be driven by the availability of internal funding. Supporting this financial constraint channel, I find that more financially constrained firms increase their investment by a larger magnitude when their health care costs decrease. Second, decreasing health care costs reduce labor costs, moving firms’ optimal frontiers outward and, under capital-labor complementarity, increasing investment. I observe an increase in firm scale and production inputs after tort reforms, providing possible support for this scaling channel. Additionally, reductions in health care costs are observed to improve workers’ health care accessibility, which can in turn enhance worker health, productivity, and improve investment. On the other hand, there is also exploratory evidence for the worker mobility channel, which predicts a positive relationship between health care costs and investment. Using relocation information for inventors across innovative firms, I find that rising health care costs reduce the likelihood of inventors departing from their original firms or joining new firms. However, baseline results show that this worker mobility channel is dominated by other forces that instead predict a negative relationship between health care costs and investment.

To further validate the negative effect of health care costs on investment, I use a second identification strategy exploring health cost increases after health insurer mergers. I exploit the fact that competition in the U.S. health insurance market varies across geographical markets, with the industry being uncompetitive overall (American Medical Association 2016). Past studies have found that a concentrated health insurance market leads to health premium rises and adverse consumer outcomes (Dranove, Gron, and Mazzeo 2003;Dafny, Duggan, and Ramanarayanan 2012;Starc 2014). As employers purchase policies or services from the private insurance market, market structure changes affect the prices employers pay for health care. This empirical exercise also relies on the fact that the U.S. insurance industry is regulated at the state level; insurers have persistent and heterogeneous market presences across states. I therefore focus on abrupt concentration changes in state-level markets induced by national-level horizontal mergers between insurers. I use changes in market concentration as an instrument for health care costs, and the first-stage regression confirms that post-merger concentration changes significantly correlate with premium prices, thereby validating the relevance criterion. The estimates from the instrumental variables two-stage-least-squares (IV-2SLS) analysis indicate that, on average, a 1% increase in health care costs decreases the investment rate of the sum of capital and R&D expenditures from its average level by 0.8%. Compared with the tort reforms setting, the insurer merger setting explores the scenario of health care cost increase, varying the insurance expense component of the cost. This additional identification strategy confirms the negative effects of health care costs on investment.

The key assumption of this second identification strategy is that insurance market competitiveness affects corporate investment only through employers’ health care costs. To satisfy the exclusion restriction, in the first place I only include mergers in which the operations of the acquiror and the target overlap in multiple states; this reduces the likelihood that the merger is intended to expand business in a particular state. Second, the driving force behind the wave of mergers during the sample period was the enactment of the Medicare Modernization Act (MMA),5which expands private insurers’ footprint in the senior care market and is not relevant to employers and their investment decisions.

Having identified the negative effects and associated mechanisms, I proceed to explore the implications of the main findings. I first look at how the composition of labor skills can change the extent to which health care costs affect investment, given the crucial role skilled labor plays in economic growth. An important fact is that high-skilled workers are more likely to enroll in employer-sponsored health plans due to their tax deductibility, making these plans more valuable for high-income earners (Gruber and Levitt 2000). As a result, firms with a higher proportion of high-skilled workers have greater health plan enrollment and consequently higher health expenditures, making these firms more susceptible to fluctuations in health care costs. By conducting subsample analysis, I find that firms in industries with higher average skills increase their investment by more when health care costs decrease. Additional analysis inInternet Appendix Ffirst shows that firms with less bargaining power relative to their insurers experience a more significant increase in insurance prices and a larger decline in investment following an increase in health care costs. Moreover, firms with greater exposure to the U.S. domestic market adjust their investment more when facing health care cost changes. When health care costs fall, firms tend to redirect their operations toward the domestic market. Taken together, these results suggest that health care costs disproportionately affect domestic firms with more high-skilled workers and lower bargaining power, which are the growth firms in the economy.

Lastly, uncertainty surrounding health care costs can also affect employers’ investment decisions. Research has demonstrated the adverse consequences of uncertainty on corporate investment (Leahy and Whited 1996;Gulen and Ion 2016;Koijen, Philipson, and Uhlig 2016). Concurrently, the volatility of health care costs is potentially a risk factor for firms, due to its effects on labor input costs and supply. With ongoing debates on U.S. health care reforms, health policy uncertainty is substantial and contributes significantly to overall health care cost uncertainty. In fact, health care is identified as the second-largest source of policy uncertainty in the United States (Baker, Bloom, and Davis 2016). Using the health care policy uncertainty index (HCPU) developed byBaker, Bloom, and Davis (2016), I find that HCPU has a negative impact on future capital investment for the period of 1985 to 2021. These results are not driven by the recent COVID-19 pandemic or health sector firms. Investment irreversibility could be the underlying mechanism, as shown by higher investment reductions for firms with higher labor displacement costs. Together, the findings provide suggestive evidence that uncertainty associated with health care costs also discourages firms’ investment.

This study contributes to the corporate finance literature on the determinants of corporate investment (Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen 1988;Lamont 1997;Rauh 2006). There are two important differences between this study and that ofRauh (2006), who examines the effects of pension underfunding on corporate investment. First, spending on health care builds human capital by improving employee health, while pensions are simply a form of deferred financial compensation. Health plan heterogeneity across firms also has implications on labor supply. Second, the funding and management of pension plans is largely within the control of the employer, who makes voluntary contributions and investment decisions. However, the cost of health care is determined by the complicated interplay between the government and various other parties in the health care system. Employers have a limited ability to manage health costs and are subject to health-care-related uncertainty.

This paper connects with various literature studying the effect of labor markets on firm value and corporate decisions (e.g.,Chen, Kacperczyk, and Ortiz-Molina 2011;Agrawal and Matsa 2013;Serfling 2016) and documents how health care costs, which are an important part of worker compensation, affect firms’ corporate policies and outcomes. Further, my paper contributes to the emerging branch of literature that examines how labor market frictions affect investment decisions (Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling 2020;Gustafson and Kotter 2022;Cho 2023). This study expands the scope of empirical work that investigates how input costs affect investment by looking beyond minimum-wage-sensitive industries. This paper also demonstrates an important relationship between health-care-related uncertainty and corporate decision-making.

Prior studies on employer-sponsored health benefits have usually focused on small businesses (e.g.,Bundorf 2002), while I look at large firms and their corporate decisions. Also, some studies look at employer health insurance mandates and their implications for firms’ labor outcomes, many of which focus on the consequences of the Affordable Care Act’s (ACA) mandate (e.g.,Dillender, Heinrich, and Houseman 2020;Almeida et al. 2022). The advantage of not using ACA as a source of variation is that ACA’s employer mandate is at the federal level and is universal for firms with more than 50 employees. In contrast, this paper explores two settings with state-level variations that induce upward and downward changes in health care costs spanning a longer time horizon.
1.1 Employer-sponsored health benefits

In the United States, employers play a crucial role in the provision of health care. Over 150 million employees and dependents are covered by employer-sponsored health benefits, which have the highest enrollment among all plan types. Medicaid and Medicare follow, covering 60 million and 40 million individuals, respectively, while ACA marketplaces and the individual market serve 20 million people. Consumers usually prefer health plans offered by their employers over ones from the individual market because employer-sponsored health benefits have pricing advantages as a result of economies of scale and favorable tax treatment. The cost of employer-sponsored health benefits has increased sharply over the past 20 years. In 2018, the average family plan premium was $19,616, up from $5,791 in 1999 (KFF 2018). Because health benefits are important tools for firms to attract and retain talent, employers—especially large ones—are reluctant to reduce benefits out of concern for firms’ reputation and employee morale. Therefore, despite the rising cost, large employers continue to offer generous health benefits. Evidence of this commitment is the stable employer contribution ratio over the past 20 years. A typical large employer contributes 80% or more of the cost for single premiums and 75% for family premiums. Anecdotal evidence indicates that employers explicitly state their contribution ratio when introducing health plan offerings to employees, such that a decreased contribution ratio reflects negatively on the employer. Although offering health benefits is voluntary, large employers rarely cancel their plans. In the period from 1999 to 2018, more than 98% of firms with 200 or more employees offered health benefits; for firms with 10–199 employees, however, the offering rate dropped from 81% to 70% (KFF 2018). In other words, large employers are not likely to adjust contribution ratios or terminate plans to buffer health care price hikes.

Employers may choose a number of methods to fund health care plans. A plan can be fully insured, self-insured, or a combination of these two plan funding methods (mixed insured). In a traditional fully insured plan, employers purchase health policies from outside health insurance companies. With self-insurance, employers pay employees’ medical claims directly, setting up tax-exempt reserves for claim payouts and making regular contributions to these reserves. Self-insured firms still engage insurance companies as third-party administrators (TPAs) for network access, plan design, and medical claim processing, but insurers earn less because they extract mark-up only through administrative services. Additionally, some employers also buy stop-loss insurance for catastrophic losses.

Firms usually offer a menu of health plan options to employees that vary in the restrictiveness of the provider network or in the ratio between out-of-pocket expenses and annual premiums. HMO (health maintenance organization), POS (point of service), PPO (preferred provider organization), and indemnity plans are the most common traditional plans. These plans vary in restrictiveness, with HMOs being the most restrictive, covering only in-network providers, and indemnity plans being the least restrictive, with no limitations on the network status of providers. Besides those traditional plans, high deductible health plans (HDHPs) gained popularity in the mid-2000s. However, “high deductibles” mean employers often need to provide financial incentives to encourage employees to choose an HDHP over traditional plans with lower deductibles and out-of-pocket caps.6In addition, larger employers usually offer lower HDHP deductibles, which increases employers’ costs for these plans. Therefore, HDHPs might not be an effective cost control device for employers.

1.2 Medical malpractice tort reforms

A tort is a civil wrong that causes harm or loss to a claimant, which results in legal liability for a person who commits such an act. Tort reform is a legislative alteration, passed on a state-by-state basis, that reduces the ability of victims to bring litigation, or limits the type or amount of damages plaintiffs can claim.Medical malpracticetort reforms seek to limit medical malpractice lawsuits and damages. Medical malpractice liability accounts for a significant proportion of health care expenditure; the cost of medical malpractice in 2008 was estimated to be a substantial $55.6 billion (Mello et al. 2010).

Within the broad category of medical malpractice tort reforms, the most common one is caps on noneconomic damages, which limit the amount a plaintiff can claim for physical pain and emotional suffering. Other common ones are caps on punitive damages, collateral source reforms, and joint and several liability reforms.7Periodic payment, split recovery, caps on total damages, patient compensation funds, and contingency fee funds are less common medical tort reforms.Avraham (2007)summarizes these tort reforms and changes in state-level status over time.

Medical malpractice tort reforms affect health care costs in two ways. First, tort reforms lower health providers’ liability costs by reducing litigation expenses and liability premiums. Past studies show that tort reforms reduce the number of lawsuits and total payouts (Avraham 2007); imposing caps on damages, especially noneconomic damages, reduces medical malpractice costs and decreases liability insurance premiums (Born and Viscusi 1998). Second, tort reforms can change how health care providers practice. Fearing financial and reputation losses from malpractice tort lawsuits, health providers may ex ante carry out excessive tests and procedures. This practice, known as “defensive medicine,” seeks to demonstrate that there has been no negligence in care and that all diagnosis and treatment options have been exhausted.Mello et al. (2010)estimate that $45.6 billion is spent on defensive medicine per year. Tort reform reduces the practice of defensive medicine by decreasing the threat of potential lawsuits, which in turn lessens the intensity of treatment and lowers the medical costs for the average patient.

Empirical studies on this topic often focus on one particular health condition.Kessler and McClellan (1996)study heart disease patients and find that tort reforms reduce medical costs without altering health outcomes. Studies on pregnancy find that caps on noneconomic damage tort reforms are associated with reduced treatment intensity (Frakes 2012), and there was no deterioration in health care quality following reforms (Frakes and Jena 2016). There is also direct evidence of the effect of medical tort reforms on employer-sponsored health premiums. Using a proprietary data set,Avraham, Dafny, and Schanzenbach (2010)find that, from 1998 to 2006, the most common set of tort reforms lowered health insurance premiums among self-insured firms.

1.3 Health insurance market competition

The U.S. health care system is characterized by its significant dependence on private insurance companies. Health insurers are important intermediaries between patients, providers, and other parties of interest, such as employers and the government. But whether the market is competitive and leads to efficient outcomes is questionable. A 2016 American Medical Association (AMA) report on health insurance market competition, used the Horizontal Merger Guidelines of the Department of Justice and Federal Trade Commission to determine that markets in 71% of 388 metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) were highly concentrated.8High concentration in the health insurance market can lead to the exercise of market power and adversely affect consumer welfare (Gaynor, Ho, and Town 2015).Dafny, Duggan, and Ramanarayanan (2012)use a proprietary data set on employer health insurance premiums, and show that the increase in local market concentration between 1998 and 2006 increased premiums by approximately 7%.Dranove, Gron, and Mazzeo (2003), in their study of local and national HMOs, find that higher competitiveness is associated with lower premiums.Starc (2014)looks at the public health insurance programs of Medigap, and draws similar conclusions about the positive correlation between market concentration and price increase.
article.2 Data:
2.1 Employer-sponsored health benefits

To investigate the relationship between health care costs and corporate investment, I first use Form 5500 welfare benefit plan data, maintained by the Department of Labor, to extract employer health plan information. The Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 requires firms with 100 or more participants on their welfare benefit plan to file a Form 5500 to report plan coverage and characteristics. A firm can have multiple benefit plans and must submit separate filings for each plan it sponsors. These filings contain information about the types of benefits offered (e.g., health, dental, vision, life insurance), the number of participants, and other plan characteristics, such as funding method. Several schedules with various purposes may be attached to the main form; the one most relevant for this study is Schedule A, “Insurance Information,” which contains insurer information and insurance expenses. A firm must attach a Schedule A form for each insurer it hires. Data are available by filing year from 1999 onward. More information about Form 5500 can be found inInternet Appendix B.

I retain the plans for health benefits and drop stand-alone dental, vision, and life insurance plans. After aggregating Form 5500 plan-level filing information to firm level, I merge the data with the Compustat universe using the employer identification number (EIN). A firm might have separate EINs for its subsidiaries, but Compustat keeps only one EIN at the consolidated firm level. I therefore manually match Compustat and Form 5500 data by company name, industry, and address. I also retrieve the subsidiary list for U.S. public firms from Bureau van Dijk and conduct matching using subsidiary names, filtering by address and industry. Financial and utility firms are excluded from the sample.

Table 1, panel A, reports the summary statistics of health plan variables from the Form 5500–Compustat–merged sample. It contains 43,660 firm-year observations, representing 5,425 unique firms from 1999 to 2016. The health insurance expenses of a firm aggregate all the insurance expenses on each Schedule A; they are set to zero if there is no Schedule A attached. The average insurance expense per participant is calculated as the ratio of total insurance expenses to the total number of participants on all insurance policies for each firm, which is used as a proxy for firms’ average health care costs in Section 3. This measure is used because employers pay for health policies and administrative services on a per participant basis. Total insurance expense changes might not necessarily represent changes in the cost of providing health care, as a rise in total expenses can result from firm expansion and an increase in employment. Another advantage of scaling total insurance expense by the number of participants from Form 5500, is the opportunity to avoid attenuation bias and the possible correlation between firms’ filing patterns and investment decisions. Only firms with more than 100 participants are required to file Form 5500; therefore, firms with participant numbers on or near the threshold enter or exit the sample through time. Also, the aggregation of total expenses and the linkage to the Compustat parent depends on whether the filings are at the individual or consolidated EIN level. If I scale the insurance expenses by a Compustat variable, such as total assets, a time-varying bias depending on firms’ reporting and participation patterns might be introduced.

The funding status—fully, self, or mixed insured—is determined by several factors, including premium per participant, third-party administrator (TPA) status, stop-loss status, and funding source (seeInternet Appendix Bfor more details). Importantly, funding status indicates to what extent insurance expenses from Form 5500 represent total health care costs. For fully insured firms, insurance expenses approximate the entire health care cost. For self-insured firms, insurance expenses do not include medical claim costs that employers pay directly from cash reserves,9and therefore are only a portion of employers’ total health care costs.Table 1shows that about 34% of the sample firms are fully insured, and about 33% are entirely self-insured.

Panels B and C ofTable 1report the summary statistics of nonmissing key Compustat variables from the Form 5500–Compustat–merged sample and the Compustat universe for the period 1999–2016. A comparison of panels B and C shows that firms in the Form 5500–Compustat–merged sample are generally larger, older, and more profitable.

2.2 Tort reforms

The first identification strategy is a quasi-natural experiment in the setting of medical malpractice tort reforms. To retrieve the list of medical malpractice tort reforms, I use the Database of State Tort Law Reforms (Avraham 2018), which reports the status of 11 medical malpractice tort reforms enacted or struck down in each state from 1980 to 2018. I focus on the most common and well-studied medical malpractice tort reform, that of caps on noneconomic damages. I also look at three other common reforms: caps on punitive damages, collateral source reforms, and joint and several liability reforms. Within the sample period of 1999–2016, 12, 9, 5, and 5 states, respectively, passed the four tort reforms of interest.Internet Appendix Table A.1shows the states with changes to tort reform, by year, from 1990 to 2016.

2.3 Health insurance market competition

The second identification strategy relies on changes in the competitiveness of the health insurance market over time. I use the information on total premiums written by each insurer to calculate insurers’ market share and the market concentration measures in each state for each year. The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) reports the top-125 insurers, by state, for total health premiums written each year. Data are available from 2004 to 2016. Each regional insurance carrier has a unique NAIC code that can be tracked over time and linked to its national parent company.

I then obtain health insurance company merger information from Zephyr and SDC Platinum. For selection criteria, I require both the acquiror and the target to be in the industry of Direct Health and Medical Insurance Carriers (NAICS code: 524114), and I restrict the search to within the United States. I include only complete deals with a deal value, if available, of greater than $10 million. Both acquiror and target must also have a valid record from the NAIC, which ensures that they have a nontrivial market share at the state level. To avoid local mergers, I also require both the acquiror and target to operate in more than one state. Targets with a single line of business in Medicaid or Medicare are excluded, because those mergers are unlikely to affect competition in the employer-sponsored health insurance market. Also excluded are targets that sold only part of their business, because this is unidentifiable in the NAIC data set. There are 14 such mergers in the period 2005–2016, as shown inInternet Appendix Table A.2. The last three columns of the table show the number of state-level markets in which the acquiror and target operate one year before the merger, respectively, along with the number of markets in which they both have a presence.

article.3 Empirical Strategies, Results, and Mec:
3.1 Correlation between firm investment and health insurance expense

I begin my analysis by looking at the correlation between health insurance expenses and investment at the firm-year level.Figure 1shows the scatter-bar plot. They-axis,Investment/Asset, is the sum of capital and R&D expenditures divided by the lagged total assets. Thex-axis shows the natural logarithm of average insurance expense per participant,log(AverageInsuranceExpense)⁠, divided into 30 equal-weighted groups; the scatter graph shows the averageInvestment/Assetin each group. The gray line shows the linear polynomial fit using the underlying raw observations. The graph indicates a positive correlation between average insurance expenses and investment.10

Fig. 1 Correlation between firm investment and health insurance expense This figure presents the scatter-bar plot of the correlation between firms’ investment and average health insurance expense. The y -axis Investment / Asset is the sum of capital and R&D expenditure divided by lagged total assets. The x -axis log ( Average Insurance Expense ) is the log form of total insurance expenses divided by the total number of plan participants using Form 5500 data. log ( Average Insurance Expense ) is divided into 30 equal-weighted groups, and the scatter graph shows the average Investment / Asset within the group. The observed positive relationship may suggest that higher health care costs can boost corporate investment; this could occur, for example, through the worker mobility channel. However, this positive relationship could also result from underlying endogeneity issues. Health care expenses might be correlated with corporate investment in multiple aspects. For instance, at the market level, a local economic boom could spur investment and increase demand for health care, thereby raising health insurance policy prices. At the firm level, an expanding company that invests heavily and offers more generous health benefits may see a rise in health care expenditures. To rectify this problem, I examine the effect of health care costs on firms’ investment exploring events that generate exogenous variations in employers’ health care costs in the next subsection.

3.2 Tort reforms

I first investigate the causal relationship between health care costs and corporate investment using exogenous variations induced by state-level medical malpractice tort reforms. As discussed in Section 1.2, medical malpractice tort reforms reduce health providers’ exposure to malpractice lawsuits, and lower the incentive for health providers to engage in “defensive medicine.” Therefore, tort reforms lessen the intensity of treatment and reduce patients’ average medical costs, and this effect translates into a decrease in employers’ health care costs. I exploit the staggered introduction of medical malpractice tort reforms that were passed state by state from 1999 to 2016, and adopt a difference-in-differences research design. The sequence and timing of state-level tort reforms are shown inInternet Appendix Table A.1and are also described in Section 2.2. I focus on tort reforms of caps on noneconomic damages, which is the most common reform with well-documented economic consequences.

The ordinary least squares (OLS) panel regression model using the standard difference-in-differences approach is shown in Equation (1) : β ϕ α λ ϵ Investmen t i t = β · TortRefor m m t − 1 + ϕ · control s i t − 1 + α i + λ t + ϵ i t . (1)

TortReformis a dummy variable that equals one if the reform is in effect in statemin yeart—1. The dependent variableInvestmentis the investment outcome (Capex, R&D expense, or the sum of the two) for firmiwhose headquarters is located in statem.11I also include firm and state-level controls to ensure that the results are not driven by firm-level time-varying factors and macroeconomic factors. Firm controls include lagged size, cash,q, PPENT, profit margin, and firm age; state-level controls include income per capita and unemployment rate. Firm and year fixed effects are included in all specifications. Standard errors are clustered at the state level for all regressions. Since the effects of tort reforms and insurance company mergers and acquisitions (M&As) in Section 3.4 are at the state level, this clustering method accounts for potential heteroscedasticity and arbitrary correlation in the error term within the local market and firm over time (Bertrand, Duflo, and Mullainathan 2004).

Previous studies have established the positive relationship between health care costs and tort reforms, a relationship that motivates this identification strategy and speaks to the relevance condition. I also validate this correlation using Form 5500 data and report the estimates for the instrumental variable (IV) version ofEquation (1)in the next subsection. The exclusion restriction requires that investment changes in states with tort reforms occur only through decreased health care costs. Besides adding firm-level and state-level controls to account for time-varying factors,Internet Appendix Dfeatures a battery of robustness tests that rule out various alternative explanations.

Table 2presents the results based onEquation (1). Columns 1–3 show the effect of tort reforms of caps on noneconomic damages on the three investment measures. In columns 1 and 2,CapexandR&Dare Compustat variables representing capital and R&D expenditure, scaled by lagged assets.Investmentin column 3 is the sum of scaled capital and R&D expenditures assuming R&D to be zero if missing.12Column 4 shows the results of using the four common reforms together as explanatory variables. The point estimate of 0.006 in column 1 implies that tort reform of caps on noneconomic damages increases the capital expenditure to asset ratio by 0.6%, which translates into a 9.4% increase inCapex, given that the average is 0.064 during the sample period. Column 3 shows that tort reforms increase total investment by 1.3%, which translates into an 8.9% increase from the average level in the sample period. Column 4 combines the four common tort reforms—caps on noneconomic damages, caps on punitive damages, collateral source, and joint and several liability reforms— and yields similar results. The sign of the coefficient of joint and several liability reform is consistent with that ofCurrie and MacLeod (2008), who find the opposite effect on physician behavior for this reform as compared with other reforms. The result is robust with the absence of controls, and when extending the sample period back to 1990.

Recent literature points out concerns about difference-in-differences research designs with staggered treatment adoption and two-way fixed effects. A potential issue is that if there are heterogeneous treatment effects across time, some observations might receive negative weights and lead to spurious identification (de Chaisemartin and d’Haultfœuille 2020). To address this concern, I utilize the efficient estimator outlined byBorusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2022)(henceforth BJS) to replicate the main analysis in a dynamic setting.Internet Appendix Cprovides details on how to construct the estimates using the procedures outlined byBorusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2022).Figure 2compares event-study estimates using the ordinary least squares (OLS) and BJS estimators. The figure shows that, estimates using the BJS approach, represented by the dashed line, are similar to those using the OLS approach. Notably, within the sample period, the majority of the tort reforms were concentrated between 2003 and 2006, sharing similar pre- and post-treatment periods. This reduces concerns about violating the homogeneity assumption when there are variations in treatment timing. Because the BJS estimates are comparable to the OLS estimates, subsequent analyses on tort reforms will continue to utilize the standard OLS approach.

3.2.1 First-stage results using Form 5500 insurance expense data

For the baseline analysis usingEquation (1), the underlying assumption is that tort reforms of caps on noneconomic damages reduce employers’ health care costs. Previous studies on medical malpractice tort reforms have established this negative relation between tort reforms and medical costs. In this part, I use the health insurance expense data from Form 5500 to verify the relation. One drawback of Form 5500 is that the data contain only the insurance expenses. For fully insured firms, insurance expenses represent a firm’s entire health care costs; however, for self-insured firms, insurance expenses are only a portion of employers’ total health care costs.13Therefore, using Form 5500 data, I expect the effect of tort reforms on health expenses to manifest only in fully insured firms, although tort reforms affect employers’ underlying true health costs regardless of funding methods.

The results of a 2SLS regression of investment on health care costs, instrumented by tort reforms, are shown inTable 3. The first three columns are the first-stage results regressing average health insurance expenses on the indicator of tort reform of caps on noneconomic damages. The independent variablelog(averageinsuranceexpense)is the natural logarithm of total health insurance expenses divided by the total number of participants covered by a firm’s plans, aggregating all the health benefit plans of a firm from Form 5500. In column 1, the sample includes all Compustat firms matched to the Form 5500 data set. Columns 2 and 3 are subsamples of firms that are and are not fully insured. Column 3 shows that, for fully insured firms, the enactment of tort reforms on average decreases health care costs by 11.1% in the period 1999–2016. Including firms that are not fully insured lessens the statistical significance and economic magnitude, as predicted. Columns 4–6 show the IV-2SLS results usingI(Tort) as an instrument forlog(averageinsuranceexpense)on the fully insured subsample. The magnitude of the 2SLS results is comparable to the M&A setting in Section 3.4 andTable 7. This set of results confirms the relevance condition. Tort reforms and employers’ health care costs are negatively correlated.

3.2.2 Robustness tests

Internet Appendix Ddescribes robustness checks on the identification strategy using medical malpractice tort reforms. I first use propensity score matching to assemble a comparable set of treated and control firms, and then conduct the baseline analysis as outlined inEquation (1)and present the results inInternet Appendix Table A.4. Second, I examine the alternative hypothesis that a state’s partisan status jointly influences investment and tort reforms, rather than tort reforms having a direct impact on corporate investment. The analysis shows that introducing an additional interaction term between a state’s partisan status and the tort reform dummy does not alter the results, and this interaction term is statistically insignificant (Table A.5). Third, for the main analyses, I use the location of a firm’s headquarters as the state in which the given tort reforms and insurer M&As (in Section 3.4) affect the firm. This approach is based on the assumption that a substantial portion of a firm’s operations and workforce resides in the state of its headquarters. To validate this approach and its underlying assumption, I examine whether geographically dispersed firms are less affected by changes in health care costs. These results are presented inTable A.6. Furthermore, I rule out the possibility that increases in investment are due to product liability tort reforms, another major category of tort reforms (Table A.7). In addition, the results also remain consistent when incorporating industry-year fixed effects (Table A.8), and are not driven by local demands (Table A.9). For more discussion and results, refer toInternet Appendix D.

3.3 Mechanisms

Having established the negative relationship between health care costs and firm investment, I further explore the mechanisms through which health care costs affect investment.

3.3.1 Scaling channel

Health benefits are offered as part of employees’ compensation packages and are therefore a component of labor cost. If labor and capital are gross complements (Acemoglu 2002), a reduction in labor costs simultaneously increases the quantity demanded for labor and capital, raising firms’ optimal scale. As a consequence of labor and capital complementarity, firms expand production and increase investment (scaling channel).

Studies have documented the gross complementarity between labor and capital at the aggregate and firm level (e.g.,Oberfield and Raval 2021). I find that the total asset growth rate increases after tort reforms (Table 4, column 1), indicating firms scale up their production after health care costs decrease. For employment growth, although the coefficient is not statistically significant,14the negative correlation between health care costs and employment changes has been documented in other studies using CPS data (Baicker and Chandra 2005) and industry-specific employee data (Lubotsky and Olson 2015). In addition, I find that the total input expenditure of production, measured by the cost of goods sold,15also increases (Table 4, column 3), further suggesting that the firm increases production and scales up when health care costs decrease.

3.3.2 Financial constraint channel

Investment is not the only margin that firms adjust after health care costs change. Columns 4, 5, and 6 inTable 4show that, besides investment, firms also increase acquisition spending, advertising spending, and receivables after health care costs decrease. This indicates that investment adjustments are not only rooted in changes in optimal production scale and production factors. Considering the concurrent increase in other expenditures and working capital, it is likely that investment adjustments also can be attributed to changes in internal financial resources (financial constraint channel).

To better understand this channel, one would need to know how firms finance their health plans. Fully insured firms pay insurance companies for health policies on a monthly or annual basis from their cash holdings. For firms with at least one self-insured plan, tax-exempt reserves are set up for future claim payouts, similar to the reserves for defined benefit retirement plans. Firms usually deposit 3 or 6 months’ worth of projected health spending into their reserves. Doing so prepares firms for catastrophic events and any miscalculations of future health spending projections. The assets in the reserve can be used to invest in financial securities. If there is a reduction in health care costs, firms pay less cash to the insurance company or contribute less to the health plan reserves. Because external finance is costly (Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen 1988;Lamont 1997;Rauh 2006), firms can use their available internal cash to increase investment.

To validate this financial constraint channel, I first check firms’ cash level after health care costs decrease.Table 4, column 7, confirms that after health care costs decrease, firms’ internal cash increases. Unreported results on firms that filed Form 5500, Schedule H,16show that both the contribution to the reserve and total expenses out of the reserve decrease following a reduction in health care costs. I then explore this channel by examining whether changes in health care costs further inhibit already financially constrained firms.Table 5shows the results. I split the sample based on how financially constrained they are, measured by whether a firm issues common dividends, by firm size, and by profit level. Columns 1, 4, and 7 show results for baseline regression usingEquation (1)for firms that do not issue dividends, are of a smaller size, and have a lower profit level, respectively, and are considered to be more financially constrained. Columns 2, 5, and 8 show the results for less financially constrained subsamples. Columns 3, 6, and 9 show the interaction exercise. To allow full flexibility, I also interact all other control variables and fixed effects with the financial constraint dummies. The dependent variable is total investment expenditure over lagged assets. The interaction terms in columns 3, 6, and 9 show that the increase in investment is 7%–8% higher (based on the sample mean of investment) for more constrained firms after the tort reforms. This analysis demonstrates that more financially constrained firms increase investment more when there is a reduction in health care costs.

3.3.3 Worker health channel

Health care costs impact investment not only by influencing firm-level allocation of inputs and financial resources, but also by affecting labor supply and human capital. If health care becomes more affordable and accessible, workers are more likely to receive the preventive services and medical treatments they need, leading to better physical and mental health (Finkelstein et al. 2012). As health is an important form of human capital (Mushkin 1962), improved employee health can reduce absenteeism and presenteeism, and increase productivity (Boles et al. 2004). Firm investment should subsequently increase.

Although directly measuring individual employees’ health outcomes is not possible here, there is evidence for improved accessibility to health care when costs decrease.Internet Appendix Table A.10looks at employers’ plan selection changes after health care costs are reduced. It shows that employers become more likely to offer indemnity plans. These plans do not restrict the specific network of hospitals and doctors that one can visit, so that employees have better access to their preferred health care providers, and are more likely to get the care they need. Looking at all columns ofTable A.10, it is possible that employers retain the most basic HMO plans, but also tend to swap out PPO plans (which have more restrictions) with the least restrictive indemnity plans when offering multiple plan options. The results illustrate a pathway for how employers improve workers’ health when health care costs drop.

3.3.4 Worker mobility channel

Health care costs reduce investment through the channels mentioned above, but they can also improve investment by decreasing worker mobility.

The availability of employer-provided health insurance is a considerable factor motivating workers to stay in the workforce, especially if they do not have access to health insurance from other sources, such as government programs or family members. This is because health insurance is much less expensive when obtained through employers than when purchased from the private market. Employer-sponsored health insurance can also discourage workers from changing jobs, as doing so would necessitate switching their health plans. Considering the diverse range of health plans employers offer and the varying values employees place on them, this leads to a phenomenon known as “job-lock” (Madrian 1994;Garthwaite, Gross, and Notowidigdo 2014), in which workers tend to remain in their current employment to avoid changes in health coverage.

Rising health care costs can hinder worker mobility across several dimensions. First, transitioning from job to job can cause gaps in coverage, as there is usually a waiting period before new employees’ coverage can start. Also, in the pre-ACA era, health plans could refuse to cover preexisting conditions for an initial period of enrollment, which could be 12 months or even longer. Rising health care costs mean that workers are likely to pay more from their own pockets during job transitions. Second, plan generosity can differ across jobs. For example, HIPAA allows employers to offer more generous plans to employees with longer job tenure, and therefore the job-changer might have to forgo their old employer’s favorable health plan. Again, the gap between benefits offered by current and new employers widens as health care costs increase. In addition, there could be changes in the sets of physicians covered and in the payment structure (e.g., for deductible, coinsurance, copayment) that change employees’ valuation of their health plans; the cost to make up for the difference is again amplified when health care costs increase. As a result of the intensified “job-lock” brought about by health care cost increases, employees are more likely to stay in their current jobs and consequently invest in firm-specific skills, in turn enhancing productivity and corporate investment (e.g.,Belot, Boone, and Van Ours 2007;Jeffers 2023).

To investigate whether health care costs discourage worker mobility, I examine a particular professional group of workers, namely, inventors, and track their mobility when health care costs change. The advantage of focusing on inventors is that they are high-skilled workers, and therefore have a higher demand for health benefits (as will be discussed in Section 3.5). Inventor information is obtained using the Harvard Business School inventor database and the USPTO patent database. Inventor mobility variables are constructed in a similar way toMa, Tong, and Wang (2022). The results inInternet Appendix Table A.11show that when health care costs decrease, inventors are more likely to leave their current firm or to join a firm as new employees. It indicates that higher health care costs can help firms retain workers and firm-specific human capital. Therefore, it is possible that through the worker mobility channel, investment can be improved after health care costs rise. However, this channel is dominated by other forces that lead to an overall negative relationship between investment and health care costs.

3.4 Additional empirical strategy using health insurance M&As

To examine how health care costs affect corporate investment, the second set of exogenous variations relies on the industrial organization of the health insurance market. As discussed in Section 1.3, prior literature finds that the health insurance market is very concentrated and that an increase in concentration is correlated with an increase in premiums (Dranove, Gron, and Mazzeo 2003;Dafny, Duggan, and Ramanarayanan 2012;Starc 2014). Therefore, shifts in insurance premiums induced by changes in health insurance market concentration can be used as a source of variations for the health care costs faced by employers.

I first examine the competition dynamics for local health insurance markets.Figure A.1in theInternet Appendixprovides a snapshot of the Herfindahl-Hirschman index (HHI) for each state in 2004, 2008, 2012, and 2016. As shown by the graphs, there is great variation in health insurance market HHIs across states and across time, and many local markets were very concentrated.Table 6, panel A, shows that the mean HHI for the state-level health insurance market is 1951.17In addition, the top-four firms in each state-level market, on average, make up 68.6% of the total market share. These figures confirm the previous findings on the insurance market’s high concentration; the changes in concentration across regions and time provide sources of variation for the analysis.

Although changes in health insurance market concentration are largely orthogonal to individual noninsurance firms, the insurance market HHI itself may not be a good candidate to instrument health care costs. This is because both local insurance market competitiveness and firms’ investment may vary with regional economic conditions. To address this issue, I look at the induced changes in concentration following insurers’ merger and acquisition (M&A) activities. The M&As involved in this empirical exercise were mainly motivated by regulatory changes to expand the Medicare market, and not driven by insurers’ intentions to capture a particular market or a few specific employers. The last part of this section andInternet Appendix Efurther elaborate how the exclusion restriction is satisfied.

To measure the changes in concentration after mergers, I build on the works of Garmaise and Moskowitz (2006) , Dafny, Duggan, and Ramanarayanan (2012) , and Ashenfelter, Hosken, and Weinberg (2015) to construct the “cumulative simulated change in the HHI,” which is the projected change in the HHI that would have occurred after the merger if all else remains the same. The purpose of using the projected change (instead of the actual change) in the HHI is to tease out post-merger market share adjustments that may correlate with local market conditions. Columns 2–4 ofTable 7present the results of the IV-2SLS analysis. Column 2 shows that the negative shock of health care costs has a significant effect on capital expenditure. Column 3 indicates that the health care cost rise induced by insurer M&A has no effect on R&D expenditure. This is probably because R&D investment requires time-to-build with a longer investment time horizon, and is therefore difficult to cut back quickly. Compared to the R&D results shown inTable 2, where R&D increases after health care costs drop, this shows that R&D investment responds asymmetrically to good versus bad news. Aggregating capital and R&D expenditures, the coefficient of –0.09 inTable 7, column 4, indicates that a 1% increase in average health care costs is associated with a 0.8% decrease in the total investment level.18

To elaborate on the magnitude of this number, I conduct a back-of-the-envelope estimate using the median firm in the sample. I start with the median employment and median total investment of 2,180 and $34.4 million respectively for the Form 5500–Compustat–merged sample from panel B ofTable 1.19I then use the Medical Expenditure Panel Survey–Insurance Component (MEPS-IC) survey for enrollment rate and average premium to calculate health care costs for this median firm.20According to the 2016 MEPS-IC survey, the enrollment rate for firms with 100 or more employees is 56%; the single premium, employee-plus-one premium and family premium are $6,499, $12,701, and $18,784, respectively, and these three types of plans contribute to 50%, 20%, and 30% of total enrollment, respectively. Therefore, an average employee costs a firm $6,397.9 for health care after taking into account enrollment rates and types. Thus, a 10% increase in health care costs is a $1.4 million increase in health costs for this median firm, and the coefficient inTable 7, column 4, predicts a $2.7 million reduction in the total investment. It is important to note that the substitution between health care costs and investment is not on a dollar-to-dollar basis, as there are other channels at play beyond the financial constraint channel (which might imply a one-to-one substitution). For example, the worker health channel affects investment via labor productivity, and the scaling channel can create a more than proportional change in investment depending on firms’ production function. These channels are discussed in Section 3.3.

3.4.1 Discussion and robustness tests

The motive for using this second identification strategy of insurer M&As is twofold. First, this approach confirms the negative relationship between health care costs and investment, by looking at a scenario where health care costs increase. In contrast, for the tort reforms setting, health care costs decrease. Second, Form 5500 reports employers’ health insurance expenses, and health care cost variations induced by insurer M&A act directly on health insurance expenses; thus, this second identification strategy enables us to obtain the magnitude of the effect of health care cost changes on investment for all firms, regardless of their plan funding method.

The key assumption for this second identification strategy is that health insurer M&As affect corporate investment only through the increases in health care costs triggered by these mergers. This makes understanding the motives behind such M&As pivotal. For the sample period, the mergers were mainly instigated by the growth of the senior care market following the enactment of the Medicare Modernization Act (MMA) in 2003. This legislation introduced a Medicare drug plan (Medicare Part D) and further facilitated the involvement of private insurers (Medicare Advantage plans). Given the vast number of Medicare enrollees and the demand for prescription drugs, the MMA prompted private insurers to respond. In fact, the majority of merger targets in this study were significant players in the Medicare market, supporting the notion that employer-related factors did not drive these mergers.Internet Appendix Eelaborates more on the MMA.

Internet Appendix Ealso discusses why these mergers are not driven by insurers’ intentions to capture a particular local market or specific employers. I conduct a robustness check that only considers mergers with more than 30 overlapping state-level markets between the acquiror and the target (Table A.12). The results demonstrate that insurers’ merger decisions, along with the subsequent impact, do not depend on a single market. In this appendix section, I also discuss why the actual change in the HHI is not suitable as an IV. Moreover, I show that the results remain consistent when considering the industry-level time-varying factors and incorporating industry-year fixed effects (Table A.13). For a more thorough discussion and additional results, refer toInternet Appendix E.

3.5 Implications

3.5.1 High-skilled firms

Firms’ exposure to health care costs depends on whether employees enroll in their health plans. From the supply side, most public firms offer health benefits voluntarily and have continued to do so after the enactment of the Affordable Care Act.21Employers cannot pick to whom to offer plans. Under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) nondiscrimination rules, employers can only differentiate between employee eligibility based on “bona fide employment-based classifications,” such as full-time status. From the demand side, even if employers offer health plans, employees do not necessarily take up the plans and become insured.22This is because health plans are not “free” in the sense that they usually require an employee contribution, and there might be trade-offs between the real wage and the health benefits. Apart from age, health status, and whether a partner has employer-sponsored health benefits, among other factors, income and skill are important in determining employee enrollment decisions.

Income level matters for health plan enrollment because employer-sponsored health benefits are exempt from federal income and payroll tax. Therefore, these benefits are worth more to employees in higher income brackets, since they reduce taxable income (Gruber and Levitt 2000).Rampini and Viswanathan (2018)argue that insurance is monotonically increasing in household wealth due to limited enforcement, so that wealthier individuals are more likely to insure future states than spend on consumption. Survey evidence confirms that the employer-sponsored health plan enrollment rate increases with wage level. For example, the 2018 National Compensation Survey from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reports that the participation rates (employer offering rate times employee enrollment rate) of health care plans among employees are, from the lowest quartile to the highest quartile of the average wage, 26%, 61%, 74%, and 82%, respectively.23In summary, high-wage workers are more likely to enroll in their employers’ health plans compared to their lower-wage peers. As a result, firms with more high-wage workers have higher health plan enrollment and spending, and would be more exposed to changes in health care costs.

I first test whether the negative relation between health care costs and investment is sensitive to firms’ average wage level. The exercise is carried out on subsamples divided by higher and lower average industry wages in the setting of medical malpractice tort reforms. I use tort reforms because those reforms affect firms’ health spending regardless of health plan funding method. I obtain industry-level wage data using Occupational Employment Statistics (OES) from the BLS for the period 1999–2016, followingBelo et al. (2017)andGhaly, Anh Dang, and Stathopoulos (2017).24OES data contain information on occupational-level hourly wages and the number of workers linked to each industry. I therefore compute the weighted average hourly wage for each industry based on its occupation distribution. The results are shown inTable 8, columns 1 to 3. Columns 1 and 2 are the subsample analysis, and column 3 is the interaction exercise. For column 3, I again interact all control variables and fixed effects with the high-wage dummy to allow full flexibility. The results show that firms with more high-wage workers adjust their investment more when health care costs change. The interaction term in column 3 indicates that the increase in total investment is 2.4% higher for firms with more high-wage workers after tort reforms, which is a 16.4% difference based on the sample mean.

Workers’ skill levels are highly correlated with wage (Murphy and Welch 1992); and, therefore, high-skilled workers also have a higher demand for employer-sponsored health care and firms who hire such workers have higher exposures to changes in health care costs. Hence, I continue to test whether firms with more high-skilled workers are affected more by changes in health care costs. Labor skill data are derived from OES data and the Department of Labor’s O*NET database. O*NET has information on the skill level ranking (job zone) ranging from one to five for each Standard Occupational Classification (SOC). I then calculate the weighted average industry-level skill index similar to the method for wage. After constructing the skill measure, I perform t-tests on subsamples of firms with different average industry skill levels and confirm that the plan enrollment ratio is significantly higher for high-skilled firms, using enrollment count from Form 5500.Table 8, columns 4 to 6, presents regression findings on how health care costs affect firms with varying labor skill levels. The interaction term in column 6 indicates that the increases in total investment are 2.0% higher for firms with more high-skilled workers after tort reforms, which is a 13.7% difference based on the sample mean. The results confirm that firms in high-skilled industries tend to increase their investments more significantly than those in low-skilled industries when health care costs decrease.

Besides having higher exposure to health care costs due to higher employee enrollment, the effect of health care costs on high-skilled firms can be exacerbated by the complementarity between high-skilled labor and capital. If high-skilled labor and capital are complementary, together substituting for low-skilled labor (Autor, Levy, and Murnane. 2003), and since high-skilled workers have a higher demand for health care, a rise in health care costs can lead to a more than proportional decrease in high-skilled labor, and in response, a greater reduction in investment.

3.5.2 Other implications

Aside from the differing reactions to health care costs among firms with different levels of skilled workers,Internet Appendix Fdelves further into the cross-sectional differences in firms’ response toward health care costs. First,Appendix F.1examines the investment responses of employers with different bargaining power relative to their insurers. The findings reveal that companies with lesser bargaining power experience a greater surge in insurance prices and a more substantial reduction in investment after insurer M&As. The results are inTable A.14.

Second, given the uniqueness of health care issues in the United States,Internet Appendix F.2examines firms’ investment responses based on their exposure to the domestic market. SeeTable A.15for the results. I find that firms with greater exposure to the U.S. domestic market adjust their investment more when facing health care cost changes. In addition, after health care costs decrease, firms tend to shift their operations back toward the domestic market. This trend is reflected in their allocation of investment, PPENT, sales, and employment within and outside the country.

Together, these findings highlight the importance of considering firm-level characteristics, such as worker skill, bargaining power, and global reach, to understand firms’ heterogeneous responses toward health care costs.
article.4 Health Care Cost Uncertainty:
It is well documented that aggregate and firm-specific uncertainty negatively affect corporate investment (e.g.,Leahy and Whited 1996;Gulen and Ion 2016). Meanwhile, health care cost uncertainty imposes risks on labor input cost and supply, and could therefore also affect investment.25Uncertainty in health care costs comes from various sources, such as government policies and regulations, advances in medical technology, market forces, and external factors affecting health conditions. This uncertainty can be substantial; for example,Baker, Bloom, and Davis (2016)find that uncertainty about health care is the second largest source of policy uncertainty in the United States, behind only fiscal policy.

Regulations can change health care provisions, market structure, and, ultimately, health care product and service prices. Therefore, healthpolicyuncertainty is an important source ofcostuncertainty, and I will focus on policy uncertainty to investigate the relationship between health care cost uncertainty and investment. For measurement, I utilize the health care policy uncertainty index (HCPU) developed byBaker, Bloom, and Davis (2016).26HCPU is 1 of 10 categorical subindices developed alongside the overall economic policy uncertainty index (EPU).27EPU is constructed by counting newspaper articles containing the keywords on “uncertainty,” “economic,” and “policy” (and their synonyms). HCPUadditionallyincludes one term related to health care policy,28using roughly 1,500 U.S. newspapers from Access World News.Figure 3shows the 3–month moving average of HCPU between January 1985 and June 2022, matching the availability of the index and capturing the effect of COVID-19. The figure confirms the significant uncertainty in health care policy, and shows that EPU and HCPU diverge from time to time.

Table 9shows the results of the analysis. The sample period is from January 1985 to December 2021, with quarterly data. Columns 1 to 3 show the results for the effect of health care policy uncertainty (HCPU) on three outcomes: capital expenditure, R&D expense, and total investment for the full sample period. Column 4 confirms the finding ofGulen and Ion (2016)on the negative impact of the overall economic uncertainty index (EPU) on corporate investment. Comparing columns 3 and 4 indicates that HCPU significantly affects total investment, exhibiting approximately half the magnitude of the effect observed for EPU.29Columns 1 and 2 show that the effect on total investment is mainly driven by capital expenditure, not R&D investment.30To alleviate the concern that the drastic increase in policy uncertainty during COVID-19 might drive the results, column 5 restricts the sample period to before COVID, dropping observations after the end of 2019. Column 6 further restricts the sample to the period from 1999 to 2016, for consistency with the main analysis in previous sections. Column 7 ofTable 9excludes health care sector firms.31Compared with column 3, column 7 shows that the effect of HCPU on investment is not driven by health care sector firms.

I then explore the mechanisms through which HCPU affects corporate investment by focusing on the potential role of investment irreversibility. Specifically, when the cost of reversing investment is high, firms have a greater incentive to delay making investment decisions in the face of uncertainty (e.g.,Bernanke 1983). Under higher levels of HCPU, labor input becomes more uncertain, and one would expect firms with greater labor adjustment costs to be more likely to delay investment decisions. To test this prediction, I explore the adoptions of thegood faith exceptionin state-level wrongful discharge laws (WDLs), which strengthen labor protection and increase employers’ firing costs (Acharya, Baghai, and Subramanian. 2013;Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling 2020). I follow the chronological list of state-level WDL adoptions inBai, Fairhurst, and Serfling (2020).32Table 9, columns 8 and 9, present the subsample analysis by sorting firms according to whether their headquarters state has already adopted the good faith exception. The findings reveal a larger investment reduction in states with WDL adoptions of the good faith exception; the difference is statistically significant by additional tests. These results show that HCPU discourages investment to a greater extent for firms with higher labor adjustment costs, and suggest that investment irreversibility could be one of the underlying mechanisms behind the observed negative relationship.

It is unlikely that the effect of health care costs on investment hinges only on the second moment (uncertainty), as the level of health care costs also matters. For example, for the second identification strategy using insurer M&As in Section 3, after the uncertainty surrounding the insurer mergers is resolved, we would observe an increase in investment if only the second moment mattered. Instead, firms decrease investment when the expected health care costs increase, suggesting that the level of health care costs plays an important role in investment decisions. Furthermore,Table 4, column 7, reveals an increase in cash holdings after tort reforms, contradicting the prediction that firms would lower their precautionary cash holdings if uncertainty were the dominating determinant.

The results in this section have important implications. Previous studies byKoijen, Philipson, and Uhlig (2016)have found that government-imposed profit risks can reduce health care sector R&D spending, andBaker, Bloom, and Davis (2016)establish a positive correlation between HCPU and investment for health care sector firms. The results in this section suggest that HCPU can also affect firms outside of the health sector. In countries in which health care is primarily funded by the government, the impact of HCPU can be mainly limited to firms within the health care industry. In the United States, however, uncertainty surrounding health care policy can significantly affect the investment decisions of non-health-sector firms. This aspect is unique and concerning, given the high degree of health care uncertainty in the United States.
article.5 Conclusion and Policy Implications:
Health care issues have received much attention in recent years from both the general public and policy makers. While employers in the United States play a crucial role in health care provision, their perspective on the impact of health care changes has been mostly overlooked. This paper highlights employers’ concerns about rising health care costs by studying the relationship between the costs of employer-sponsored health benefits and firms’ corporate investment decisions. Using plausibly exogenous variations in health care costs induced by state-level medical malpractice tort reforms and health insurer mergers, I show that changes in the health care costs faced by public U.S. firms negatively affect their capital expenditures and R&D spendings. The effect is especially prominent in domestic firms with more high-skilled workers and in firms with lower bargaining power relative to insurers. Uncertainty in health care policy also discourages capital investment.

Based on this paper’s findings on the connection between health care and corporate outcomes, several general policy implications can be drawn out. First, controlling health care expenses not only addresses health care issues but also promotes investment. Second, the cost of health care varies greatly across the nation (as seen, e.g., in the Dartmouth Atlas Project), and this tends to influence firm-level investment choice and operations, similarly to state taxation (Giroud and Rauh 2019). For example, businesses in high-cost regions may struggle to provide competitive wages and benefits to skilled workers, which in turn affect their investment, productivity, and competitiveness. Policy makers could address this by supporting initiatives that reduce regional disparities in health care costs, such as increasing access to telemedicine. Third, mandates for covering various health conditions can lead to investment consequences. For example, South Dakota mandates the coverage of autism treatment in its Essential Health Benefits benchmark plan for 2022 onward. Given the potential cost impacts, policy makers need to consider the trade-offs in providing more comprehensive health coverage and minimizing the financial burden on employers. Fourth, my findings indicate that young firms with more skilled workers are especially vulnerable to health care cost shocks, which could discourage them from investing and expanding their business. Policy makers should consider measures to assist these high-growth small businesses in managing their health benefits, for example, by providing targeted subsidies or tax credits. Lastly, policy makers should recognize that uncertainty around health care regulation can also have unintended impacts on non-health-sector firms’ investment decisions. In summary, the findings suggest that controlling health care costs and promoting investment are interconnected goals that policy makers should take into consideration.
